issue_title: NgramWordAnalyzer and PorterStemmer are not independent?
issue_body:
Based on feedback from CS410 instructor  Ismini Lourentzou I am reporting the following issue regarding the interaction of Ngrams and Stemmers in MeTA.

Briefly: should they not be ***_independent_** ?  In that case the  **order** should not matter.

However in MeTA the order does result in different results - consider:

    stemtok = metapy.analyzers.Porter2Filter(previousTokenizer)
    stemtok.set_content(doc.content())
    ng3ana = metapy.analyzers.NGramWordAnalyzer(3,stemtok)
    character_trigrams = ng3ana.analyze(doc)

The above yields slightly different results than the following:
    
      ng3ana = metapy.analyzers.NGramWordAnalyzer(3,stemtok)
      character_trigrams = ng3ana.analyze(doc)
      stemtok.set_content(doc.content())

Notice in the second case that the stemmer has occurred *after* the NGram. 

To better understand the intended concept of independence vs dependence -   consider a different pair of analyzers that *should* be dependent: the  stemmer and the  length analyzer. Swapping *those* analyzers that would definitely impact the result: e.g.  the stem of derivation and derived should be the same - maybe deriv ?  In that case if the length<=5 rule were applied before  the stemming then the word would disappear altogether: but after stemming it is retained.
 
But I do not perceive any fundamental reason why stemming and ngram generation should have any mutual effect /interactions: they should be independent,  no?  If they were independent then order should not matter.  But it does here. So then can we get some insight from the developer(s) why these two features actually do interact - and why?

issue_title: config: reasonable defaults
issue_body:
The null config file looks like so:

```
 - 
    repo: ''
    sha: ''
    hooks: 
        - 
            id: ''
```

Suggestions for defaults:
- top-level list:  [ repo: pre-commit-hooks ]
- repo: this is the one thing that should in fact be required I think.
- sha: default to 'master'  (allow branch names)
- hooks: default to all-from-that-repo
  - each hook should define their own default enabled state (with default-default on)
  - allow users to override their enabled state to on/off

This makes it much easier to maintain the configuration file, allows pre-commit to do something useful with zero configuration (need to install policy to keep pre-commit-hooks language agnostic and most-common-denominator), and allows the configuration to grow organically in very small increments.

issue_title: Should releaseManifold or clearManifold reset btManifoldPoint::m_appliedImpulse to 0?
issue_body:
I have been experiencing some issues where I create a stack of boxes that slides along the floor. If I reset the scene (by deleting all rigid bodies) just as the stack of boxes is falling and then recreate the stack, the new stack immediately falls over. I believe that this is caused by reusing released `btManifold`s.

Deleting the rigid bodies causes `btCollisionDispatcher::releaseManifold` to be called. I guess that this adds the manifold back into the object pool. Before doing so, it calls `clearManifold`, which calls `clearUserCache` on every contact point in the persistent manifold. It looks like `m_appliedImpulse` is not reset to zero at any point.

issue_title: Change flow control for writers
issue_body:
If `writer.write()` a coroutine -- no need for internal writer buffer at all.
On socket's internal send buffer overflow the writer should be paused without trying to fill application level transport buffer -- it makes no sense but wastes extra memory and CPU cycles.

issue_title: Accessing Response.text can fail with an AttributeError
issue_body:
## Long story short
Handling of payloads in the server response is not consistent and can lead to `AttributeError`s since code depends on methods that may not exist. I will try to create a PR that fixes this issue.

## Expected behaviour
We handle payload setting consistently and don't fail when `text` is called after setting a string payload using the `body` property setter.
 
## Actual behaviour
The setter for `Response.body` has support for automatic type detection via the `PayloadRegistry`. This means that in the above example, the body will be encoded as a `StringPayload`. When trying to access this via the `text` property we fail because `StringPayload` does not have a `decode` method.

When you set `text` directly any string is decoded to `bytes` and set to `_body` directly without passing through the `PayloadRegistry`.

## Steps to reproduce
```python
>>> from aiohttp.web_response import Response
>>> r = Response()
>>> r.body = 'asdf'
>>> r.text
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/private/tmp/.ve/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/web_response.py", line 539, in text
    return self._body.decode(self.charset or 'utf-8')
AttributeError: 'StringPayload' object has no attribute 'decode'
```

## Your environment
Reproduced on aiohttp 2.3.10 and 3.1.2

issue_title: Problem with trailer header
issue_body:
## Long story short
I described an issues in https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1619 Later I update aiohttp for new version however the problem is still here.

Http response (body and trailers part):
"{ "doc": "some data"}\nX-Custom-Header1=0\r\nX-Custom-Header2=custom2\r\nX-Custom-Header3={}\r\n\r\n

## Expected behaviour
aiohttp loads json body and skips trailers. Parsing of the next response should not read any trailers from previous response.

## Actual behaviour
aiohttp loads json and do not read any trailer data from reader. Debugging shows that response reader buffer contains this trailer data.
When I use the same session the next response parsing will be started from the trailers. Therefore it leads to parsing exception.
## Steps to reproduce
1. Use the same session for requests
2. Get response from server with trailer headers (as shown above)  
## Your environment
macOS Sierra, aiohttp 2.0.7

issue_title: RFC: Add "publish_github_prerelease" add-ons
issue_body:
### Overview

This add-on allows to automatically publish pre-release on GitHub. 

A pre-release is identified by a git tag and is associated with one or multiple packages (python wheels, installers, archives, ...)

Project maintainers are responsible to graduate a "pre-release" into "release".


###  Use cases

#### Automatic upload of python wheels associated with  [ITKPythonPackage](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage) project

##### Steps

Every day a *bot* will:
* update ITK description and package version in  `ITKPythonPackage` (see details below)
* commit the changes to `ITKPythonPackage`. 

Then, each CI services (or dedicated build bots) will:
* checkout `ITKPythonPackage` source
* build the associated wheels
* use `publish_github_prerelease.py` to publish the generated wheels as pre-release

##### `update_python_version.py` script

This script is not (yet) part of `scikit-ci-addons` and will live in the `ITKPythonPackage` project repository and can be executed as a standalone script. For example, it could then be downloaded and executed by the *bot* named  [kwrobot](https://github.com/kwrobot).

**Usage**

```
Usage:
  python update_python_version.py /path/to/last/ITK/source
```

**Steps**

The *bot* will:
* clone the latest `ITK` source, checkout `nightly-master` branch
* clone `ITKPythonPackage`
* download the script
* execute the script
* commit and push the change to `ITKPythonPackage`

**Script description: update_python_version.py**

Given the patch to both an `ITK` source tree and an `ITKPythonPackage` source tree, the script will:
* get the local commit date of the `HEAD` of [ITK@nightly-master](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/tree/nightly-master). (Using a command like `git show -s --format="%cd" --date=local`)
* get `<major>.<minor>.<patch>` (By extracting the value associated with variables `ITK_VERSION_MAJOR`, `ITK_VERSION_MINOR` and `ITK_VERSION_PATCH` using script like [CMakeParser.py](https://github.com/Slicer/Slicer/blob/eefeac9286f48552e8418a11f412b2823a09b407/Utilities/Scripts/SlicerWizard/CMakeParser.py))
* update [CMakeLists.txt](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage/blob/master/CMakeLists.txt) replacing the ITK version associated with `GIT_TAG` CMake variable to match the latest commit associated with [ITK@nightly-master](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/tree/nightly-master)
* update [setup.py](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage/blob/master/setup.py) by replacing version<sup>1</sup> `<major>.<minor>.<patch>.dev<YYYYMMDD>` where `<major>.<minor>.<patch>` corresponds the ITK release version and `<YYYYMMDD>` corresponds to the date of the `ITK@nightly-master` commit

<sup>1</sup> To facilitate the update of the version, it could be added to a separate file like `itkVersion.py` or `itk/version.py`

**example of execution**

On unix:

```
# TODO: Delete old virtual env and create a new one (e.g name itk-nightly-wheel)

pip install scikit-ci-addons
git clone git://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage
cd ITKPythonPackage

platform=$(ci_addons publish_github_release --current-platform) # manylinux1, macosx or win
commit_date=$(ci_addons git --commit_date)

./scripts/dockcross-manylinux-build-wheels.sh && \
ci_addons publish_github_release  \
  --clear-pattern "*$platform*.whl" \
  --keep-pattern "*dev$commit_date*.whl" \
  InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage \
  "dist/*dev$commit_date*$platform*.whl"
```

On windows:

```
$PYTHON_DIR="C:/Python27-x64"
$env:PATH="$PYTHON_DIR;$PYTHON_DIR/Scripts;$env:PATH"

# TODO: Delete old virtual env and create a new one (e.g name itk-nightly-wheel)

pip install scikit-ci-addons
pip install ninja
git clone git://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage
cd ITKPythonPackage

$platform=(ci_addons publish_github_release --current-platform) # manylinux1, macosx or win
$commit_date=(ci_addons git --commit_date)

# Adapted from http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2251622/conditional-execution-and-in-powershell#7272878
$(python setup.py bdist_wheel | Out-Host;$?) -and `
$(
  ci_addons publish_github_release  `
    --clear-pattern "*$platform*.whl" `
    --keep-pattern "*dev$commit_date*.whl" `
    InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage `
    "dist/*dev$commit_date*$platform*.whl"
| Out-Host;$?)
```

issue_title: /v1/search endpoint handle's TLDs inconsistently.
issue_body:
If I search the /v1/search endpoint for a fully qualified ID featuring the '.id' TLD, the search will fail--for instance try these searches:
- https://core.blockstack.org/v1/search?query=alexc.id
- https://core.blockstack.org/v1/search?query=judecn.id
- https://core.blockstack.org/v1/search?query=aaron.id

If I strip the '.id' TLD from those searches, the endpoint returns results:
- https://core.blockstack.org/v1/search?query=alexc
- https://core.blockstack.org/v1/search?query=judecn
- https://core.blockstack.org/v1/search?query=aaron

However when I search for a fully qualified ID featuring any other TLD that doesn't end in '.id' (e.g. '.id.blockstack'), the endpoint does return results:
- https://core.blockstack.org/v1/search?query=batzdorff.id.blockstack
- https://core.blockstack.org/v1/search?query=pie_head.id.blockstack

issue_title: Running into some resource allocation problems
issue_body:
Since there is no general help forum for Toil or Toil scripts and it is in heavy development, I thought this would be the best place to ask. I have been working on porting over my existing pipeline work to Toil due to its broad support for Mesos. I am currently running this pipeline just locally. I wanted to do something a little different because I re-use many of the same methods in different pipelines so all of the methods live in their own project and are then imported into the pipeline script where I construct the workflow and graph using wrapJobFn() to define all of the jobs and then use the addJob() method to chain them together for maximum flexibility. You can see this in this repo: https://github.com/dgaston/ddbio-ngsflow.

All of my wrapper methods are in ngsflow and the workflow-* scripts are the various pipelines. workflow-somatic-amplicon.py is the current one I am testing.

The problem I am having is that the jobs don't seem to be respecting the resource allocations. For instance I am specifying --maxCores of 4, in my configuration input I specify that BWA should use 4 cores and so that is passed to the cores keyword argument in wrapJobFn(). However when executing I have four different BWA jobs, each multithreaded to 4 threads running (4 different samples) as well as two other jobs executing. It was my understanding that in this case there should only be a single BWA job executing at a time, as each BWA job should be requesting 4 cores out of a max of 4 cores allowed to toil. I see similar issues with memory usage.

Any help would be appreciated.

Regression: price_excl_tax is necessary for availability
issue_body:
I get to upgrade an old Oscar project, and that went really well. Thanks for keeping Oscar so backwards-compatible! I noticed just a small regression for a corner case:

I work on a project where it was essential that Oscar stores the tax-inclusive price and the price without tax is calculated. The reasoning is that the tax-inclusive prices are legally binding, and doing it this way ensures that a bug in the price calculation logic has less of an impact.

This was easily done by storing the price_incl_tax on the stock record, and writing a short pricing policy for it. But two recent changes [here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/commit/82d9e7d2647ab50aacd653d21f93f8423f974fbf#diff-38aa6a43fa33c6bad17543acc1e24541) and [here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/commit/3e4dda9a89a51323ea50d216bd895101aa014bbf#diff-38aa6a43fa33c6bad17543acc1e24541) make this harder, because they look at a `price_excl_tax` and assume because that is not set, the product is not available to buy. 

I'd like to argue that pricing and availability are concepts that should be kept separate, and the two commits linked above essentially bypass Oscar's pricing logic. 

My workaround was to backport the `StockRequired` policy from Oscar 1.4.

issue_title: Issue with London time during ambiguous time.
issue_body:
Evidently the `fromutc()` algorithm for `tzfile` is not working quite right for some zones, as first reported by @jreback on pandas-dev/pandas#14631

```python
>>> from dateutil import tz
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> LON = tz.gettz('Europe/London')
>>> dt = tz.enfold(datetime(2013, 10, 27, 1, 30, tzinfo=LON))
>>> str(dt)                               # Proper behavior
'2013-10-27 01:30:00+00:00'
>>> str(tz.enfold(dt, fold=0))    # Proper behavior
'2013-10-27 01:30:00+01:00'
>>> str(dt.astimezone(tz.tzutc()).astimezone(LON))  # Moves us forward one hour!
'2013-10-27 02:30:00+00:00'
```

This will probably be solved when we implement our own `fromutc()` for `tzfile` (which is on the to-do list anyway). Compare to the behavior of a zone in PST:

```python
>>> from dateutil import tz
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> PT = tz.gettz('US/Pacific')
>>> dt = tz.enfold(datetime(2011, 11, 6, 1, 30, tzinfo=PT))
>>> str(dt) 
'2011-11-06 01:30:00-08:00'
>>> str(tz.enfold(dt, fold=0))
'2011-11-06 01:30:00-07:00'
>>> str(dt.astimezone(tz.tzutc()).astimezone(PT))
'2011-11-06 01:30:00-08:00'
```

issue_title: Subtask: Tablet article page
Extend widget class to support custom context
issue_body:
As of now, the only data that widgets are capable of rendering is that which comes from their defined fields. After thinking about how to best implement the event widgets, I realized that it would be useful to allow for additional data (not from widget fields) to be passed to a widget template. 

For example, I would like to build a widget that automatically fetches 10 upcoming events and renders them in a list. Our current design allows us to define a widget that displays 10 events, but each of those events would have to be picked manually. To get around this, we should extend the widget functionality to allow us to write a bit of code that will fetch 10 events from the database and pass them to the template.

Here's how I imagine it looking:

```python

from dispatch.apps.events.models import Event
from dispatch.theme.widgets import Widget
from dispatch.theme.fields import EventField

def EventsWidget(Widget):

    id = 'upcoming-events-widget'
    title = 'Upcoming events widget'
    template = 'widgets/upcoming-events.html'

    featured_event = EventField('Featured event') # field to display a featured event

    def context(self, data):
        """
        This is an optional method that can be defined on any widget. 
        It simply allows you to add additional data to the template context before rendering. 
        The output of this function is what is passed to the template for rendering.
        """

        # The `data` argument is a dictionary containing data from the event fields. 

        # Additional data fields can be added before returning the final context
        data.upcoming = Event.objects.filter(is_published=True).order_by('-start_time')

        return data
```

- [x] Add support for optional `context()` method on widget classes. Its default implementation should not change the data
- [x] Add tests to make sure `context()` method works as expected

issue_title: Nested excludes get overwritten
issue_body:
It seems whenever an parent level exclude is specified with dot notation, the child field level excludes are overwritten.  It would be great if they could be concatenated.

Here's an example:
```
from marshmallow import fields, Schema

class FooChildSchema(Schema):
    boo = fields.String()
    far = fields.String()
    faz = fields.String()

class FooSchema(Schema):
    foo = fields.String()
    bar = fields.String()
    baz = fields.String()
    child = fields.Nested(FooChildSchema, exclude=('boo',))

obj = {
    'foo': 'foo',
    'bar': 'bar',
    'baz': 'baz',
    'child': {
        'boo': 'boo',
        'far': 'far',
        'faz': 'faz',
    },
}

serialized = FooSchema(exclude=('foo', 'child.faz')).dump(obj).data
print(serialized)
```
I expect :
```
{'child': {'far': 'far'}, 'baz': 'baz', 'bar': 'bar'}
```
instead, I get:
```
{'child': {'far': 'far', 'boo': 'boo'}, 'baz': 'baz', 'bar': 'bar'}
```

issue_title: Improve performance of solarposition.ephemeris
issue_body:
`solarposition.ephemeris` returns results in dataframe with a localized time index, rather than UTC time. This results in slow performance because the dataframe is assembled from Series and np.arrays that are calculated using UTC time, requiring a timezone conversion.

**To Reproduce**
Change line 693 from
`DFOut = pd.DataFrame(index=time)`

to 

`DFOut = pd.DataFrame(index=time_utc)`

to observe the speedup: roughly 23 sec calculation time for 500k timesteps with `index=time`, and <5 seconds for `index=time_utc`

 - ``pvlib.__version__``: 0.5.2
 - ``pandas.__version__``:  0.20.3

Recommend that `solarposition.ephemeris` return a dataframe indexed in UTC time.

issue_title: Organize Lasagne test suite
issue_body:
While finishing the release, I've noticed some deficiencies with the way the test suite is currently set up:

1. `python setup.py test` doesn't work.
  * For one, the requirements for that are listed in `setup.py` under  `extras_require` instead of `tests_require`. The disadvantage of putting them under `tests_require` is that those dependencies are installed to the current directory if they're not available, but we advise our users to use the `requirements-dev.txt` for that anyway, so probably nobody knows about `extras_require['testing']` either.
  * And secondly, `setup.py test` doesn't know that it should run pytest. That would be easy to add: https://pytest.org/latest/goodpractises.html#integration-with-setuptools-test-commands.
2. `lasagne/conftest.py` is installed along with Lasagne. [On Thursday night](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/375#issuecomment-130826943), I was able to convince setuptools to include the tests in the source distribution (via `MANIFEST.in`), but still not install them (via `include_package_data=False`). It's impossible to stop it from including `lasagne/conftest.py`, though, because it looks like it's part of the lasagne package. This file tells pytest's doctest plugin to unconditionally ignore the doctests in `corrmm.py`, `cuda_convnet.py` and `dnn.py`.
  * Do we actually want that? I mean, *unconditionally*? They're not run locally either now, are they?
  * Is it possible to place that configuration somewhere else, so it's not installed along with lasagne?
3. I don't see how it's possible to test the lasagne version that's installed. We tell users to run `py.test` from the `Lasagne` checkout. Thus, it will always import `lasagne` from the checkout, not the installed version.
  * Maybe that's good for contributors who didn't install Lasagne in development mode (because we're forcing them to test their current version of the code, not what they installed). So maybe let's leave it like that?
  * The alternative would be to (optionally?) install the test suite along with lasagne, such that it can be run via `python -m lasagne.tests` or maybe `python -c 'import lasagne; lasagne.tests.run()'`. Numpy has something like that as `python -c 'import numpy; numpy.test()'`.

issue_title: arm mode cannot be switched when using arch.asm()
issue_body:
Thanks for the keystone integration.
I have just notice one things:

the thumb parameter which I personally prefer over the function variants (capstone/capstone_thumb) cannot be changed once `self._ks` is initialized
```python
    def asm(self, string, addr=0, as_bytes=False, thumb=False):
        ...
        if self._ks is None:
            if thumb:
                self.ks_mode += _keystone.KS_MODE_THUMB
            else:
                self.ks_mode += _keystone.KS_MODE_ARM
            self._ks = _keystone.Ks(self.ks_arch, self.ks_mode)
        encoding, count = self._ks.asm(string, addr, as_bytes)
        return encoding
```

something like this would do if thumb parameter is to be kept
```python
    def asm(self, string, addr=0, as_bytes=False, thumb=False):
        ...
        mode = _keystone.KS_MODE_THUMB if thumb else _keystone.KS_MODE_ARM
        ks = _keystone.Ks(self.ks_arch, self.ks_mode|mode)
        encoding, _ = ks.asm(string, addr, as_bytes)
        return encoding
```

issue_title: Join user and package area files
issue_body:
Currently the `parse_area_file` function only allows one single file. This causes limitations in software like `satpy` where we may want to have a "user" area file and a "package" area file. The easiest/cleanest way to handle this is to make a pyresample allow more than one file. This is even more useful for the new yaml files which could allow users area files to "update" existing information in the "package" area file.

I propose that there be a new function (for backwards compatibility) that accepts one of the following input formats for the areas files:

1. single area file name
2. single file-like object
3. single file content string
4. iterable of area file name or file-like object

issue_title: Race condition in monitoring when copying jobs
issue_body:
In a clean Ganga session with a completely wiped `~/ganga_dir`, running the following:

```python
j = Job(); j.submit(); j.copy(); j.copy()
```

causes an exception in the monitoring loop about 80% of the time. The exception given is:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../ganga/python/Ganga/GPIDev/Lib/Job/Job.py", line 611, in updateStatus
    self.time.timenow(str(newstatus))
  File ".../ganga/python/Ganga/GPIDev/Lib/Job/JobTime.py", line 127, in timenow
    be_statetime = j.backend.getStateTime(childstatus)
  File ".../ganga/python/Ganga/Lib/Localhost/Localhost.py", line 112, in getStateTime
    j = self.getJobObject()
  File ".../ganga/python/Ganga/GPIDev/Base/Objects.py", line 1025, in getJobObject
    raise AssertionError('no job associated with object ' + repr(self))
AssertionError: no job associated with object <Ganga.Lib.Localhost.Localhost.Localhost object at 0x314c650>
```

Pausing for a second between after the `submit()` operation makes the race condition disappear:

```python
from time import sleep
j = Job(); j.submit(); sleep(1); j.copy(); j.copy()
```

This came up when converting the JIRA1961 test to the new system.

issue_title: Make menu sidebar visibility toggle with a button.
issue_body:
# Feature request

I thought it would be a nice idea to be able to toggle the sidebar menu visibility on and off with a button.

Here is a toy example of what I mean: 

* http://jsfiddle.net/hThGb/1/

Many web apps have this functionality in order to save screen space.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4594825/23351899/b5796200-fc89-11e6-800d-e484bd25e72a.png)
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4594825/23352113/ec88304a-fc8a-11e6-98a1-9934026e10fc.png)


In this example we can see that the maps goes way to much to the right, even when changing the size of the text input fields:

```python
class RouteUbicationInline(SortableInlineAdminMixin, TabularInline):
    model = RouteUbication
    extra = 2
    show_change_link = True

    formfield_overrides = {
        models.CharField: {'widget': TextInput(attrs = {'size': '20'})}
    }
```

Without django-jet, using the normal admin and the snippet above, I am able to visualize all the fields including the map without having to use the horizontal scroll.

I hope I can soon get a one time license from Jet in order for this to be considered, or even maybe try to implement this myself if I get some time.

issue_title: Fast Redirect To Top Frame
issue_body:
When in framed mode and opening the replay frame (`mp_` modifier), the redirect may take too long, as it requires wombat.js to be inited and 'onreadystatechange' event to be fired.

Add a 'fast-redirect check' to top frame in `head_insert.html` to optimize the redirect to the top frame.

issue_title: Demag GUI main function
issue_body:
This commit breaks running Demag GUI for the pip install: 8b9229c

When Demag GUI is running from pip, __name__ will not be "__main__".  Getting arguments from sys.argv needs to be in the "main" function.  You can see how it is done in Pmag GUI, which is compatible with both pip and developer installs.  

In my commit 374b6b0 I was trying to leave the structure as you had it with your 'main' function in , but I think it would really be better to change it to the style of Pmag/Magic GUIs.

issue_title: Proposal: Dropping SendGrid *v2* legacy API support
issue_body:
Anymail switched to use SendGrid's newer v3 API in February, 2017 (Anymail v0.8). But the older SendGrid v2 API remained available as an option.

In the interest of reducing maintenance overhead, I'm proposing dropping support for SendGrid v2 in a near-future release.

If you are using Anymail with SendGrid v2 and cannot switch to the default v3 API, please comment. (This affects you if your Django settings have `EMAIL_BACKEND = "anymail.backends.sendgrid_v2.EmailBackend"`.)

In particular, if you must use SENDGRID_USERNAME/SENDGRID_PASSWORD authentication, rather than SENDGRID_API_KEY as required by their v3 API, I'd like to better understand that use case before finalizing this decision.

issue_title: [2] Get mypy running on your codebase with clean output.
issue_body:
http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/

This usually requires just adding type annotations for empty global data structures. Back in January, this took a few hours of work (including reporting bugs with reproducers). It’s probably even less work now. By default, mypy will only check the interior of functions that are annotated, so with an unannotated codebase, this is just making sure mypy can analyze your entire codebase.

issue_labels: [{'id': 277074172, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20acme', 'name': 'area: acme', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 210831837, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20cert%20management', 'name': 'area: cert management', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 146954787, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/bug', 'name': 'bug', 'color': 'fc2929', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
CentOS 6 and CentOS 7

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
For CentOS6 (certbot-auto):  https://certbot.eff.org/all-instructions/#centos-6-none-of-the-above
For CentOS7 (yum):  https://certbot.eff.org/all-instructions/#centos-rhel-7-none-of-the-above

Currently using certbot 0.14.1 on CentOS 7 and certbot-auto 0.16.0 on CentOS 6.

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
certbot certonly --manual--cert-name cubs --domains chicago-cubs.venafi.example,cubs.venafi.example

_(note: non-Let's Encrypt ACME server specified in /etc/letsencrypt/cli.ini)_

```
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log
Starting new HTTPS connection (1): tpp.venafi.example

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Confirm that you intend to update certificate cubs to include domains
[u'chicago-cubs.venafi.example', u'cubs.venafi.example']. Note that it
previously contained domains [].
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
It appears that certbot is unable to parse domains from the previous certificate when it has no CN and the DNS SANs are a critical extension.  Symptom is the unexpected prompt shown above (applicable [previous_cert_pem.txt](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/files/1137352/previous_cert_pem.txt)).


## Background
Venafi has introduced an ACME server interface in Trust Protection Platform 17.2 and the common application involves using certbot to obtain certificates from an enterprise PKI.  Microsoft's Active Directory Certifiate Services (ADCS) is a very popular enterprise PKI and when it enrolls a certificate request that lacks a Subject DN (has only SANs) the subjectAltName (2.5.29.17) extension is always critical.  This behavior is consistent with [Section 4.2.1.6 of RFC 5280](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5280#section-4.2.1.6) which states "If the subject field contains an empty sequence, then the issuing CA MUST include a subjectAltName extension that is marked as critical."

issue_title: Extremely simple http-01 in apache
issue_body:
How about implementing `http-01` for Apache? After writing #1268 I realized that the authenticator would not even have to touch users config - just drop a file in `/etc/apache2/conf-enabled/` with the following contents:

```
<IfModule mod_headers.c>
    <LocationMatch "/.well-known/acme-challenge/*">
        Header set Content-Type "application/jose+json"
        DocumentRoot /var/lib/letsencrypt/apache-http01-root
    </LocationMatch>
</IfModule>
```

reload the daemon, drop necessary resources in `/var/lib/letsencrypt/apache-http01-root`, and voila!

The biggest advantage is that we might even get away without read access (parsing) of the existing configs, not even mentioning write access :) That means less scope for errors (a lot of users don't even get to the authorization stage, because of MisconfigurationErrors raised in the parsing step).

Possible problems:
- users don't have `/etc/apache/conf-enabled` by default
- `DocumentRoot` seems to be allowed there (https://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.2/mod/core.html#documentroot), but if it's not, then we can drop the stanza and try to read the config file and find existing `public_html`

@jdkasten?

issue_title: Highlighting for trailing-newlines needs improvement
issue_body:
As of PR #151, ColorReporter and HTMLReporter, when encountering a `trailing-newlines` style error, highlight the number of the line on which the error was made, and provide surrounding context lines in grey as usual. However, as per @david-yz-liu's suggestion, the reporters should show/highlight "*all* the trailing newlines (including the last one) to make it more obvious what the problem is." 

There are two problems with this proposition:
1. It seems that the Python `readlines` method automatically omits an end-of-file newline if there is one, so that in a file with 2 trailing newlines, the `_source_lines` will only have 1 stored; in a file with 3 trailing newlines, there will be 2 in `_source_lines`; and so on. 
_Possible workaround:_ assume there is always 1 more trailing newline than reported, and go from there.
2. In a file with more than 2 trailing newlines (so more than 1 extra), the checker finding trailing-newlines errors only identifies the last newline (really the second-last, see problem 1) as erroneous. Thus, highlighting _all_ extraneous newlines is not feasible at the reporter level. This seems to be a fix better suited to a checker or patch.

issue_title: Requests as MutableMapping
issue_body:
Requests should possible expose a `MutableMapping` interface, rather than `Mapping`.

Although we present immutable interfaces on the request properties, it'd make sense for requests to more correctly match the `scope` interface, and allow dict mutation.

We'd need to take some care to invalidate cached properties if we did so, particularly if `request['headers']` is mutated.

issue_title: Enforcement of server restrictions by SoS
issue_body:
Our cluster has a no-large-job-on-head-node policy but we will have to use head nodes for all data communications (`rsync`) and job preparation, submission etc.

In practice it is easy to adopt a one-server-multi-config approach like this

```
hosts:
    pbs_head:
        address: cluster_address
        queue_type: process
    pbs:
        address: cluster_address
        queue_type: pbs
        template_file: pbs.tmpl
        ....
```

and use these two queues as follows

```
sos run script rsync_step -q pbs_head
sos run script real_step -q pbs
```

The difference is that task from the first command would be executed directly on head node, and the second command would be submitted to PBS.

The convenience of submitting jobs easily to the cluster also makes it easy to submit the jobs incorrectly. For example,

```
sos run script real_step -q pbs_head
```

would easily slow down or even crash the head node.

I am therefore wondering if there can be some safe guard against this kind of incorrect usage. For example, in the `pbs_head` definition, we could define `walltime` or `mem` or something similar to kill the task if they exceed the limit. This is actually not difficult to do because we can code this in our pulse thread and let the pulse thread kill the job when needed.

issue_title: Starting processes by hand.
issue_body:
To debug crashes, it is often useful to start each of the processes (the plasma store, the plasma manager, the local scheduler, the global scheduler, redis, etc) by hand. This way, some of the processes can be started in gdb, and logging messages from the different processes are easier to read.

Currently, this can be done as follows (run all of these commands in separate terminal windows).

1. Start Redis
```
rm dump.rdb; ./src/common/thirdparty/redis/src/redis-server --loadmodule src/common/redis_module/ray_redis_module.so
```
2. Start the global scheduler (and pass in the Redis address)
```
./src/global_scheduler/build/global_scheduler -r 127.0.0.1:6379
```
3. Start the plasma store
```
src/plasma/build/plasma_store -s /tmp/s1 -m 1000000000
```
4. Start the plasma manager
```
src/plasma/build/plasma_manager -s /tmp/s1 -m /tmp/m1 -r 127.0.0.1:6379 -h 127.0.0.1 -p 23894
```
5. Start the local scheduler
```
src/photon/build/photon_scheduler -s /tmp/sched1 -p /tmp/s1 -m /tmp/m1 -r 127.0.0.1:6379 -a 127.0.0.1:23894 -h 127.0.0.1
```
6. Modify `start_ray_local` in `lib/python/ray/services.py` to be something like this.
```python
def start_ray_local(node_ip_address="127.0.0.1", num_workers=0, num_local_schedulers=1, worker_path=None):
  if worker_path is None:
    worker_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)), "workers/default_worker.py")
  redis_port = 6379
  redis_address = address(node_ip_address, redis_port)
  object_store_names = ["/tmp/s1"]
  object_store_manager_names = ["/tmp/m1"]
  local_scheduler_names = ["/tmp/sched1"]
  address_info = {"node_ip_address": node_ip_address,
                  "redis_port": redis_port,
                  "object_store_names": object_store_names,
                  "object_store_manager_names": object_store_manager_names,
                  "local_scheduler_names": local_scheduler_names}
  # Start the workers.
  for i in range(num_workers):
    start_worker(address_info["node_ip_address"],
                 address_info["object_store_names"][i % num_local_schedulers],
                 address_info["object_store_manager_names"][i % num_local_schedulers],
                 address_info["local_scheduler_names"][i % num_local_schedulers],
                 redis_port,
                 worker_path,
                 cleanup=True)
  return address_info
```
7. Then start Python, and run
```python
import ray
ray.init(start_ray_local=True, num_workers=2)
```
Then try running some commands and see which processes crash! Processes can be started in gdb as well (or lldb on Mac).

issue_title: Feature request: preserve leading/trailing spaces
issue_body:
Netconan replaces multiple whitespace with a single space in order to make regex maintenance and correctness easier. However, it might be nice to preserve leading (and trailing?) spaces so that files "look the same" on the input and the output.

Especially if anonymizing indented structures such as router configuration dumps, Python, JSON, etc.

So right now, things like custom commands get handled by delete delay by us actually parsing to see if it was a CC,
What if we had a red specific event which cogs could trigger when they are finished with a message which is handled through on_message as if it were a command for things like it? issue_title: How is kernel_initializer take effect in Conv2D function
issue_body:
In the faster rcnn example, threre is usage of `Conv2D` as:
https://github.com/tensorpack/tensorpack/blob/d2c5cc162aaf15b4f9077fb8370ed4b98ee8b848/examples/FasterRCNN/model_fpn.py#L48-L50
but in `Conv2D` function, there is no argument of `kernel_initializer`:
```
@layer_register(log_shape=True)
def Conv2D(x, out_channel, kernel_shape,
           padding='SAME', stride=1,
           W_init=None, b_init=None,
           nl=tf.identity, split=1, use_bias=True,
           data_format='NHWC'):
    """
    2D convolution on 4D inputs.

    Args:
        x (tf.Tensor): a 4D tensor.
            Must have known number of channels, but can have other unknown dimensions.
        out_channel (int): number of output channel.
        kernel_shape: (h, w) tuple or a int.
        stride: (h, w) tuple or a int.
        padding (str): 'valid' or 'same'. Case insensitive.
        split (int): Split channels as used in Alexnet. Defaults to 1 (no split).
        W_init: initializer for W. Defaults to `variance_scaling_initializer(2.0)`, i.e. kaiming-normal.
        b_init: initializer for b. Defaults to zero.
        nl: a nonlinearity function.
        use_bias (bool): whether to use bias.

    Returns:
        tf.Tensor named ``output`` with attribute `variables`.

    Variable Names:

    * ``W``: weights
    * ``b``: bias
    """
    in_shape = x.get_shape().as_list()
    channel_axis = 3 if data_format == 'NHWC' else 1
    in_channel = in_shape[channel_axis]
    assert in_channel is not None, "[Conv2D] Input cannot have unknown channel!"
    assert in_channel % split == 0
    assert out_channel % split == 0

    kernel_shape = shape2d(kernel_shape)
    padding = padding.upper()
    filter_shape = kernel_shape + [in_channel / split, out_channel]
    stride = shape4d(stride, data_format=data_format)

    if W_init is None:
        W_init = tf.variance_scaling_initializer(scale=2.0)
    if b_init is None:
        b_init = tf.constant_initializer()

    W = tf.get_variable('W', filter_shape, initializer=W_init)

    if use_bias:
        b = tf.get_variable('b', [out_channel], initializer=b_init)

    if split == 1:
        conv = tf.nn.conv2d(x, W, stride, padding, data_format=data_format)
    else:
        inputs = tf.split(x, split, channel_axis)
        kernels = tf.split(W, split, 3)
        outputs = [tf.nn.conv2d(i, k, stride, padding, data_format=data_format)
                   for i, k in zip(inputs, kernels)]
        conv = tf.concat(outputs, channel_axis)

    ret = nl(tf.nn.bias_add(conv, b, data_format=data_format) if use_bias else conv, name='output')
    ret.variables = VariableHolder(W=W)
    if use_bias:
        ret.variables.b = b
    return ret
```

issue_title: [Usage Question] How to write a ModelDesc that is partially from a meta_graph file.
issue_body:
Suppose I have some meta graphs and associated checkpoints, and I want to initialize part of my model from them, how do I do this within tensorpack? E.g., I want to construct the my first five layers from the meta_graph (from input to layer five), find the tensors of the layers using get_tensor_by_name, and add more functionality to the model using these layers. 

My specific question is on how to use InputDesc/ModelDesc in tensorpack for placeholders that are to be loaded from meta graphs, because I do not want to construct these placeholders, and they are to be loaded from meta_graph file. 

I noticed that there is a function called inputs() in ModelDesc, but it constructs the placeholders on a temporary graph. The meta graphs can be large, so initializing the meta graphs on the temporary graph just for the placeholders is undesirable

issue_title: How to write it: RPC client with out of order responses
issue_body:
This is a fun little problem and I was wondering what is the best way to use trio to solve it. I hope this is appropriate here.

Consider a simple RPC protocol:

Example request: `{"id": 15353, "params": ...}`

Example successful response: `{"id": 15353, "result": ...}`

Example error response: `{"id": 15353, "error": ...}`

The client can send multiple requests concurrently. The server can respond out of order.

Suppose we want to write a client for this protocol using trio. We want to send many concurrent requests and handle their responses. We want to have a synchronous-looking API, e.g. `try { response = await send(request) } except { ... }`.

---

Here is how I would solve it (I've hidden it in case someone wants to come up with an independent solution 💭):

<details>

- Add a future/promise abstraction using `trio.Event` internally. Curio has a [simple one](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/blob/bd0e2cb7741278d1d9288780127dc0807b1aa5b1/curio/promise.py).

- The sending function creates a future, stores it in a `request id -> future` dict, sends the request, and waits on the future.

- Spawn a received task which receives responses from the server and sets the result/exception on the corresponding future in the `request id -> future` dict.

- For each request, spawn a task which would use the sending function.

</details>

Is there a more elegant way?

issue_title: raven doesn't like LOCALS_KEY_KI_PROTECTION_ENABLED
issue_body:
```py
import trio

async def main():
  from raven import Client
  client = Client(SENTRY_DSN)
  raise Exception

if __name__ == '__main__':
  trio.run(main)
```

The cause is:

```pybt
Error in sys.excepthook:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/raven/utils/json.py", line 48, in dumps
    return json.dumps(value, cls=BetterJSONEncoder, **kwargs)

    **kw).encode(obj)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 199, in encode
    chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 257, in iterencode
    return _iterencode(o, 0)
TypeError: keys must be a string

issue_title: couple of suggestions post-tutorial
issue_body:
1) Alternative to log or sqrt for "potential_method" - a nice compromise could be the [inverse hyperbolic sine function](http://mathworld.wolfram.com/images/interactive/ArcSinhReal.gif). Continuous from neg. inf. to pos. inf. and exhibits logarithmic properties asymptotically - therefore log-scales values without need for imputation. 

    - Others in the bioinformatics field have begun using this transformation, e.g: [Michael Hoffman](https://twitter.com/michaelhoffman/status/707252800359686144) at U. Toronto. 

2) Mysterious cell in the "remove rare cells" cell - probably is just a mistake?

     - `genes_keep=sum(data)>10;  data=data(:,genes_keep); genes=genes(genes_keep)`

3) Alternative method for getting users introduced to method - have the tutorial be housed in a Google CoLab setting, i.e: https://colab.research.google.com/. This would remove any barrier to installation as a user goes through a first pass. Of course, afterwards, they would still need to install it and all that that entails. Distill.pub uses CoLab notebooks to allow interested readers to do exactly this - https://distill.pub/2018/building-blocks/.

What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?

Comment 1 by eik...@gmail.com



Comment 2 by eik...@gmail.com

A file with @prefix isn't in the 'nt' format, which is a very restricted line-based 
format for RDF. Your file has a confusing extension, and I confirm that the source 
page is being misleading by showing "N-Triples with @prefix".

I added a note about this in the rdflib parser class:

% pydoc rdflib.syntax.parsers.NTParser

    class NTParser(rdflib.syntax.parsers.Parser)
     |  parser for the ntriples format, often stored with the .nt extension
     |  
     |  See http://www.w3.org/TR/rdf-testcases/#ntriples
